name: The Economist
philosophy: An inspired interpretation of The Economist's design language — the British weekly newspaper of record built on a signature 1.5cm red top bar (Economist Red #E3120B), a literary slab-serif (Milo Serif) for headlines, an elegant humanist sans (Officina Sans) for ledes and metadata, and a strict editorial discipline: byline removed (every article unsigned), section eyebrows in red caps, drop caps on feature opens. The brand reads as 200-year-old global authority.

design_language.motion_signature: Almost no motion — 200ms ease-out on hover; signature is the subtle vertical-divider sweep on section dividers and the red-bar persistent header that never animates.

trademark_signals: Economist Red (#E3120B) — the signature top-bar red, used in a 1.5cm red strip running the full page width as the masthead band, and on section eyebrows + section dividers
Milo Serif slab-serif for every headline at every tier — weight 700 with -0.02em tracking, sizes 28–56px — the brand's typographic identity
No bylines — articles are unsigned (a 200-year editorial tradition); only section + date appear at the article head
Drop caps on the opening paragraph of feature articles — large red Milo Serif initial cap floats 3 lines deep
Section eyebrows in red small-caps Officina Sans — 'Britain', 'United States', 'Finance & economics', 'Science & technology' — uppercase tracking +1.5px
ai_slop_avoided: Don't add bylines. The Economist's unsigned-article tradition IS the brand discipline
Don't substitute Milo Serif with Times or Garamond. The slab character is the brand's typographic signature
Don't drop the masthead red bar. The 1.5cm red strip IS the most-recognized brand cue
Don't introduce a second chromatic color. The brand is red + black + cream/white only
Don't round corners on cards or images. The brand is strictly rectangular
